content:
# The Weight of Keys

She found them in the third drawer, beneath a folded dish towel that still smelled faintly of cedar. Three keys on a brass ring — one for the deadbolt, one for the mailbox, one for a lock she had never been able to identify.

She turned them over in her palm. The brass ring had worn a pale groove into the largest key, the way water carves stone, slowly and without intention. She pressed her thumb into that groove and felt how perfectly it fit, how many years of the same small gesture had made that hollow.

The apartment was mostly empty now. Rectangles of slightly brighter paint marked where the pictures had hung. The radiator ticked in the corner, cooling down or heating up — she could never remember which direction made that sound.

She set the keys on the kitchen counter. They made almost no noise at all.

Outside, a bus passed and the windows shivered in their frames, just barely, the way they always had. She had stopped noticing that shiver years ago and now she noticed it again, the way you notice a word you have said so many times it briefly becomes strange.

She picked up the keys once more. Put them down.

The unknown key caught the afternoon light and held it for a moment — dull gold, patient, keeping its secret the way objects do when there is no longer anyone left to ask.
